What do you mean by "special name", the XXX part or the leading underscore? If you're talking about the XXX, I had assumed that the `p_` functions had to be named the same as their nonterminal. If that's not the case, I can remove the XXX.

If you're talking about the underscore, it's required so the function doesn't get treated as a rule directly. If you omitted the underscore, you'd have to either rename or delete the template method to keep this from happening, which means you'd need different behavior when first instantiating a CParser.

This arises because I wrote `_create_param_rules()` in a similar way as `create_opt_rule()`, which is called during `__init__()`. What makes more sense to me is to instead use a very simple metaclass so this modification (or creation) of methods only happens at class-creation time, not every time a parser is instantiated. The metaclass's `__new__()` method simply loops through the class members, modifies them as described, and passes the modified classdict to `type()`.

I had a look at replacing `tempdir` with the pytest's `tmpdir` fixture as you suggested and I liked the idea.

The pytest's `tmpdir` fixture is an instance of the `py.path.local` class. Once I finish converting all the test cases to `pytest`, the `zookeeper` and `kafka` fixtures will automatically get a `tmpdir` fixture from `pytest` and will pass it to the `ZookeeperFixture` and `KafkaFixture` constructors, respectively.

Before the conversion is complete, though, the constructors` will still not being passed `tmpdir` as a parameter and will have to instantiate it internally.

Hence, this import is only needed temporarily to allow the `unittest` code to continue working while we're going through this conversion process. Once everything is converted to `pytest`, we can remove this since the `tmpdir` will always be provided by the `pytest` fixtures.

> Accessory objects should call set_value with should_callback=False and should_notify=True?

It really depends on your use case to be honest. I had discussion with people for this before, but we couldn't figure a better way for it. For example, if your accessory wants to turn on the light, it can just call `set_value(new_value)` to whatever is necessary (with `should_callback=True, should_notify=True`, both are the default) and then leave it to the `setter_callback` to actually turn the bulb on. On the other hand, if turning the lights CAN ALSO happen by some external action that you detect after it's done, then the above approach breaks down, because you have to have more complicated control logic.

The problem is how to handle both cases above well enough. So far I have found these flags acceptable in the cases I've seen, but then what have I seen? If you think the API is missing stuff or if you have a suggestion, I would be more than happy to discuss it and make changes.

I think it's a bit of a leap to assume that `varnames` is *public* just because it's on of the few functions in `pluggy` that doesn't have a leading `_` ;)
Imho we should be able to define functions with normal names and assume they're for internal use *unless* exposed through `__all__`.
That being said I can add the wrapper but I do think this kind of stuff should start being split up into multiple modules; eg. a `utils` module in this case.

@RonnyPfannschmidt regarding vendoring in pytest I **really** think we should be running the `pytest` test suite as part of our CI process in `pluggy` since it is the major dependent project. 
What do you think about me making an issue for that?
I feel that if we get that in place we can work towards a much tighter release process between the two projects where `pytest` can use latest `pluggy` if available and fail over to the vendored version. This would of course mean that `pytest` **does not** require `pluggy` as a default dependency.

Because if you specify nothing, then it should be enabled (by default). The flag behavior should do the opposite of what "not specifying" does.  To compare:

**enable behavior**
not specified -- enabled
flag specified -- enabled
enabled true -- enabled
enabled false -- disabled

**disable behavior**
not specified -- enabled
flag specified -- disabled
disabled true -- disabled
disabled false -- enabled

I'm fine with either since you can accomplish your scenario either way, but I just don't like that the flag behavior is the same as the default (which is counter to the idea of flags)

Yeah, explicitly clearing the env in general is nice because it means we're more consistent/reproducible.

It probably doesn't matter either way here, but for things on the build path which we cache (possibly even outside of the local machine) things (like invoking gcc), stripping the entire env, and adding whatever we need explicitly, is a nice safe approach to try to ensure reproducibility. It also means that we can think to explicitly add those env vars to any cache keys we may need to (or, with v2 process execution, this happens by default, so it ensures that we actually get cache hits across users, because otherwise things like `$HOME` will always be different! :))

Has nothing to do with this particular change, but setting `TimeoutInMinutes` to the stack config `timout`, which uses seconds as unit confuses two different timeouts: The stack config `timeout` defines how long cfn-sphere should keep polling for events, where `TimeoutInMinutes` defines how long CloudFormation itself waits for the stack operation to finish.

`"TimeoutInMinutes": stack.timeout / 60` would be correct, but an incompatible change for many. I would just not set it, as the default timeout is not defined but depends on the type of resources used: http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/troubleshooting.html

Right now you're doing high-level logic inside low-level connection logic. The dispatcher is the place where we should be denying inbound requests. You can do this while also keeping the registered handlers.

- Put dispatcher in a state where it starts returning declines
- Call close on all of the peers we want to kill
- Connection makes a note of its outstanding requests/responses and returns a future that resolves when all of those are done
- A timeout callback actually kills the connection after some period of time

One of the motivations of the `Namespace` abstraction is that it gives us a good place to inject alternate logic for endpoint naming.

Example: we've talked a bit about having low-level and high-level APIs (for the same resource). If we had such a thing for a delete operation, we'd currently end up with **two** endpoint names called `foo.delete`, which Flask will not allow. The path forward is to give the `Namespace` another member variable and generate different endpoint names based on some prefix (e.g. `x.foo.delete` and `y.foo.delete`).

The individual resource functions all get called with an argument of the form `foo_id` because this is how we build our URI patterns (e.g. `/api/v1/foo/:foo_id`).

The resources themselves have an `id`; this translates between the two since we're patching with a list of resources and don't have individual URIs to extract the `foo_id` from.

>I don't see the point of this base class. There don't seem to be any sub-classes of it other than AtmosphericScreen. Is there a plan to extend this in a different direction so eventually there will be another subclass of this, where this abstraction might be relevant?

A few updates.  I just finished adding an `OpticalScreen` class, which mimics the functionality in optics.py.  (Though with an extra feature: aberrations can now go to arbitrarily high Zernikes!)  

After looking at the result and reading through (http://stackoverflow.com/questions/3570796/why-use-abstract-base-classes-in-python) I decided to just use duck-typing instead of an ABC and deleted the `PhaseScreen` ABC entirely.  This does raise a question related to style though:

`OpticalScreen` is time independent, which means there's no reason to define methods `advance`, `advance_by`, or `reset` that are present in `AtmosphericScreen`.  This needs to be handled somehow by `PhaseScreenList.advance`, `advance_by`, and `reset` methods though.  I could think of three options:

- Define `OpticalScreen.advance()` as a noop, either directly or via subclassing (this was the former approach I just deleted).
- Use a `hasattr(screen, 'advance')` inside `PhaseScreenList.advance()`.
- Use a try/except in `PhaseScreenList`.  I.e., [EAFP](https://docs.python.org/2/glossary.html#term-eafp)

The latter seemed the most pythonic, so I went with that.

There's also a new `Aperture` class that handles obscurations and struts similar to `optics.py:generate_pupil_plane()`.  I haven't reimplemented `optics.py:load_pupil_plane()` to load pupil planes from images yet, but that's on the todo list.

I didn't bother with the other stuff in `optics.py`, like the `otf`, `mtf`, `ptf`, and so on.  These aren't imported into the galsim namespace by `__init__.py`, so I'm not sure if these really need to be reimplemented in the new framework or not.

And finally, I'm considering renaming the `path_difference` method to `wavefront`, which is probably a bit more standard.  It does conflict conceptually with the use of `wavefront` in `optics.py`, however.  In `phase_psf.py`, I would want `wavefront` to represent something like the number of nanometers that the physical wavefront leads or lags the ideal plane/spherical wave.  In `optics.py`, however, `wavefront` already includes the next steps needed to create the PSF, namely division by the wavelength and exponentiation.  Roughly: `optics_py_wavefront = np.exp(2j * np.pi * joshs_preferred_definition_of_wavefront / wavelength)`.

Any comments regarding implementing MTF, PTF, OTF, etc. and renaming `path_difference` to `wavefront` would be welcome!

The Platform library (and thus  the platform provided/required otPlatLog() function) is is *common* to both the ncp and the cli - it does not know which function to call/invoke.

That's why we have these outputs,

NONE = the bit bucket no output

DEBUG_UART - the common debug uart scheme

APP_STREAM - as defined by the application layer (ie: CLI or NCP, or in the future a SENSOR MODULE, or THERMOSTAT module) - maybe this should be called APP_PROVIDED

PLATFORM_PROVIDED - is for situations like Nordics RTT - we at TI have a diffferent solution that works with our tool chain and thus it is really "platform level"

POSIX - is really sort a special case of platform provided

hmm this is a little complicated because there are two separate behaviors. continuous/contiguous_dump controls whether the DumpProfile() is atomic -- whether at the end of the call, the function "closes off" the json and that's a discrete piofile file.  One could periodically save multiple profiler files over time.  That is the "old way".  
The "new way" is with continuous dump, where the file is closed off only on shutdown, thus making only one file possible.  One could use dump_period to automatically dump, or else call the dumps manually (although they would still be one continuous file).

To merge these two config items, I would need to discard the old behavior (one discrete profile file for each Dump() call). Is that desired?
